title: Mutual Friction in Dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii Thermal Counterflow Turbulence
authors: Kyo Yoshida, Hideaki Miura, Yoshiyuki Tsuji
abstract: We report numerical simulations of the dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii equation for a bulk region of thermal-counterflow turbulence. Quasistationary states are obtained over a range of forcing, damping, and healing-length parameters. The mutual-friction acceleration exhibits cubic scaling with the mean relative velocity between the superfluid and normal-fluid components, and the coefficient of this scaling is linked to the phenomenological damping parameter. The intervortex spacing follows the expected dimensional scaling in the weak-forcing regime. Comparison with a straight-vortex-line model suggests that the vortex-line orientations are nearly isotropic.
summary: This study uses numerical simulations of the dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii equation to characterize turbulence in quantum fluids. It discovers a cubic scaling law between mutual friction and relative velocity and demonstrates that the vortex tangle is nearly isotropic. The results provide a bridge between phenomenological damping parameters and observable turbulent dynamics.
main_problem: Investigating the dynamics of thermal-counterflow turbulence in quantum fluids, specifically focusing on the relationship between mutual friction, relative velocity, and vortex-line density.
main_result: The mutual-friction acceleration exhibits a cubic scaling with the mean relative velocity (f proportional to w^3), with a scaling coefficient linked to the damping parameter gamma, and the vortex tangle is found to be nearly isotropic.
method: Numerical simulations of the dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii equation (DGPE) using a Fourier transform method on a 1024^3 grid.
model_or_system: A bulk region of thermal-counterflow turbulence modeled by the dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii equation (DGPE), representing a system where superfluid and normal-fluid components move in opposite directions.
key_observables: Mean relative velocity (w), mutual-friction acceleration (f), momentum density (p), intervortex spacing (l), and the ratio of observed to model-predicted acceleration (A).
important_parameters: Damping parameter (gamma), healing length (xi), external acceleration (f), and the regime where xi is much smaller than the intervortex spacing (l) and system size (L).
main_assumptions: The DGPE treats the condensate and non-condensate as superfluid and normal-fluid components, which is an oversimplification for liquid 4He.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the cubic scaling of relative velocity versus acceleration for different parameters; Fig 2 provides a visualization of the vortex tangle; Fig 3 shows the mean intervortex spacing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of thermal-counterflow turbulence, describes the DGPE numerical method and simulation setup, presents results regarding scaling laws and vortex isotropy, and concludes with a comparison to experimental 4He values.
why_interesting: It provides insights into non-equilibrium dynamics and dissipation in quantum fluids, which is relevant to understanding turbulence and energy dissipation in many-body systems and ultracold gases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Relative velocity w versus acceleration f. Data from the same parameter set (γ, ξ) are connected by dashed lines as a guide. Nearly overlapping dashed curves correspond to the same value of γ. The values of γ are 0.025, 0.05, and 0.1 in decreasing order of w. Solid lines show fits to the scaling law (2).

FIG. 2. Isosurface of n = 0.25¯n in a quasistationary state for ( ˜f, γ, ξ) = (0.8 × 10−4, 0.025, 0.016). The external forcing points from left to right. The visualization data were pro- duced using VISMO [20].

FIG. 3. Mean intervortex spacing l versus acceleration f. Data from the same parameter set (γ, ξ) are connected by dashed lines as a guide. Nearly overlapping dashed curves correspond to the same value of γ. The values of γ are 0.025, 0.05, and 0.1 in increasing order of l. Solid lines show fits to the scaling law (3).

FIG. 4. Ratio A of the observed acceleration f to the model value fmodel in quasistationary states. Data from the same parameter set (γ, ξ) are connected by dashed lines as a guide. The vertical axis is multiplied by γ for visibility. Solid lines indicate A = 2/3 for γ = 0.25, 0.5, and 1 in increasing order.
